critic_raw:
This ensemble vs. ARIMA retail forecasting experiment has several foundational methodological problems.

1. 12-MONTH LSTM LOOKBACK WINDOW OVERLAPS WITH THE ARIMA TRAINING WINDOW IN A WAY THAT CANNOT BE DISENTANGLED. The LSTM uses sequences of 12 prior months as input. With training on months 1-36 and test starting at month 49, the LSTM at test time uses months 37-48 as its lookback -- a period that falls entirely within the validation window. This means the LSTM implicitly uses validation-period data as part of its test-time input, whereas ARIMA uses only the univariate series through the end of training. The comparison is not equivalent: the LSTM has access to 12 months of data (months 37-48) at test time that the ARIMA baseline does not see as training data in the same way.

2. MAPE IS AN INAPPROPRIATE PRIMARY METRIC FOR RETAIL REVENUE FORECASTING WHEN REVENUE CAN BE NEAR ZERO. MAPE divides by actual values; when monthly revenue is very low (e.g., off-season months, store closures, promotions that shift revenue to adjacent periods), MAPE becomes undefined or explosively large. The design does not describe whether any months have near-zero actual revenue or whether such cases are excluded. If excluded, the effective evaluation set is systematically different from the full deployment scenario. If included, MAPE will be dominated by low-revenue months rather than reflecting average forecasting accuracy.

3. EQUAL-WEIGHT ENSEMBLE OF XGBOOST AND LSTM IS NOT TUNED AND CANNOT BE THE OPTIMAL COMBINATION. The ensemble output is the equal-weight average of XGBoost and LSTM predictions. This is presented as the treatment model, but equal weighting has no theoretical or empirical justification -- it is a default choice. The relative predictive accuracy of XGBoost vs. LSTM on this dataset is unknown before the experiment, so equal weighting may heavily upweight a worse component. A validation-set-tuned ensemble weight would be more defensible, but was not used.

4. ARIMA BASELINE GRID SEARCH OVER AIC IS NOT COMPARABLE IN EFFORT TO THE NEURAL MODEL TUNING. ARIMA parameter selection via AIC on the training set is computationally trivial and does not use the validation set. XGBoost and LSTM hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set. This creates an asymmetry: the neural ensemble uses validation information for hyperparameter selection while ARIMA does not. The stated 'equal tuning effort' claim is not operationally defined and likely does not hold.

5. DETRENDED REVENUE FEATURE IS FIT ON TRAINING SET BUT APPLIED TO TEST -- THE LINEAR TREND ASSUMPTION MAY NOT HOLD. The design fits a linear trend on months 1-36 and subtracts it from all values including test. If the actual revenue trend is nonlinear (exponential growth, structural break post-month 36), the detrended test values will be systematically biased -- the residuals will contain the nonlinear trend component, which the model will misinterpret as signal. The ARIMA model, which can include differencing to remove trends, may handle non-stationarity more robustly than the linearly-detrended feature set fed to XGBoost and LSTM.
all_issues_raised: LSTM lookback window at test time includes validation-period data not equally available to ARIMA baseline
MAPE undefined or unstable for near-zero revenue months; exclusion creates a selection bias in evaluation set
Equal-weight ensemble is unjustified; optimal weighting is unknown a priori and not validated
ARIMA tuned via AIC without validation set while neural models tuned on validation set -- asymmetric tuning effort
Linear detrending assumption on training data may produce biased test features if true trend is nonlinear or exhibits structural breaks
Only 60 monthly observations; LSTM training on 36 observations is insufficient to learn seasonality; hyperparameter search on 12 validation points is near-random
Linear detrending applied globally may create systematic residuals in test period if true trend is nonlinear; preprocessing assumptions leak training-period structure
ARIMA baseline receives no features while ensemble receives rich engineered features; comparison tests feature richness not architecture
MAPE undefined or unstable for near-zero revenue months; handling of extreme events not specified
Fixed equal-weight ensemble may underperform best component if models have unequal predictive power on this small dataset
60 monthly observations yields only ~24 effective LSTM training sequences after windowing — insufficient for stable learning or hyperparameter tuning
MAPE is dominated by low-revenue seasonal troughs, producing unstable test estimates that do not reflect overall forecasting quality
Equal-weight ensemble is not justified and likely suboptimal given the different strengths of XGBoost and LSTM across seasonal regimes
Linear detrend fit on training data may not match validation/test trend structure if revenue growth is nonlinear
AIC-based ARIMA selection is an in-sample criterion that may overparameterize for short time series forecasting tasks
